So welcome everybody to the Momentum Builder Workshop Lean Edition live Q&A. This is for cohort number two. It's November 11th, 2021. If you're in the United States, thank you and your veteran. Thank you for your service today as veterans that in the US. We have three people submitted questions in advance and then we'll jump to questions that are submitted on the call if anybody has any additional questions. So feel free to ask as we're going to have some time. All right, the first questions are two are from Pramav. I will read the question. Yeah, what I may do is, I think we've done this, we did this with the art of email live workshops. What I'm going to do is I'm going to drop the question into chat so everyone can see it as we ask and answer these questions, but I'll read it to you just so we have it. All right, here's the question. Sean and Andre, in this course, you have taught us the vertical customer journey. We can also call this a direct marketing path. Example, Facebook ad to MPPS, to email series to sale, but someone like Seth Godin has been a proponent of the horizontal customer journey. We can call this the brand marketing path where the word spreads from person to person. In other words, your brand is a purple cow. Is there any specific reason that you have focused on the vertical path and not focused so much on the horizontal one? Do you want to do this one first? Do you want me to do this one first? How do you want to handle this one? Yeah, I mean, there's so many ways to come at this one. Yeah, I mean, these are clearly two different approaches to exposing an audience to a brand. And this course is the one path, but it's not in exclusion of the other one. I'm not sure if you're in auto-bmail, I think you are. You certainly seen all of other posts around world building, which is our way of doing more of the brand marketing side. It's creating a world, I suppose, that can be seen as a brand, as a mini-brand, macro-brand. So it is not an either-all sort of scenario. We do both, but this particular training is a direct approach because that's the way to monetize attention. So it's certainly the fastest way. So that's probably queued up sure, nicely. You can tell I'm sort of at the edge of my seat. I don't know that I even understand the point of this question. I'll just be honest. So it's like, here's probably a lousy analogy, but I'll try this one. I have a 2019 Toyota car, a 2019 Toyota Tundra out in my front yard, a big pickup truck if you're not familiar with it. So I bought that truck because I wanted a truck. At no point have I been in the truck and thought to myself, this isn't a sports car. Damn it. Of course it's not. Now, that doesn't mean, I also want an Audi S3, but it doesn't mean that sports cars can't exist because I chose to buy a pickup truck or the reverse. It's just, it's a different tool for a different job. So I don't, this question is confusing to me because it's like, you're asking why we taught the course, the way we taught the course, which is exactly the way we describe the course. At no point did we ever suggest that this was a brand marketing course. It's not, the Momentum Builder Workshop is designed to solve a very specific problem, which is to prevent procrastination. It's really easy to procrastinate across two dimensions. One is, I don't know what I should be doing. And then the other is, I know what I should be doing, but I don't know how to do it. Those are two dimensions of procrastination. And that's the problem that Momentum Builder Workshop was designed to solve. Where it's like, let's figure out the shortest path, not exactly the shortest path, but from our perspective, the shortest viable path between a prospective customer and a potential sale. Now, we could arguably say it's not the shortest path because we could just go add to sales page. That's the shortest path. And that's fine, but that's not the Momentum Builder Workshop. That doesn't line up with the things that we teach. So the idea of the workshop is to say, how do we connect an audience with an offer and the shortest number of steps that we feel good about in the way that we teach, and that eliminates the two dimensions of procrastination that are so common. Because when you, we make it very clear, these are the things that you need to do. And then within each of those things, we say, this is how you do it. That has nothing to do with Seth Godin's brand marketing journey. There's just, there's no relationship to that. We're comparing pickup trucks to sports cars all of a sudden. Both of them are great in the different uses, but that's not what this course is. So I would caution everybody to not intellectualize this. Don't look for the exceptions. Don't try to fit this into some grand scheme. Just recognize what the Momentum Builder Workshop is all about, which is to build momentum, to break the inertia that's so common that we, and we see it so often that someone wants to take value out into the world. And they're not quite sure what they need to do to do that. And then when they realize what they need to do, they're not quite sure how to do it. So it's, we wanted to shorten that distance. We wanted to clarify things so that in a reasonable amount of time, three weeks, four weeks, five weeks, 10 weeks, whatever makes sense for you, but in a reasonable amount of time, you could go from A to B. Now you may want to layer on all kinds of sophistication later. And that's fine, but that's not what this workshop is about. This workshop is about A to B, preventing procrastination along the way. I hope that makes sense. I felt like a rant. I'm just going to rant all day today. I'm just going to say that's my plan. That wasn't, that wasn't a rant. That wasn't sweet. All right. Next question. I think, you know, I think the most valuable thing that I can share in relationship to this question is something that I observed working with clients and I call it, I think there's an, there's actually a, I think I'm misusing this name, but I'll, I'll tell you the name that I use for it, and then explain why I call it that the name I use is management by exception. I think that's actually a real term like there's a, and I, but I don't know. I don't, that's not what I'm talking about. So let me explain to you what I mean, because I think it's showing up for you Pranav and I want to, I want to help you not do this anymore. Because I think if we can help you solve this issue, we can accelerate your progress infinitely faster. So here's what I observed a lot. This is, it wasn't half of the clients I worked with, but it was some significant number, let's say 30 or 40% of the clients I worked with, I worked with almost 250 clients over the years. But some significant percentage of clients. Well, let's look at, let's look, let's compare both. One significant percentage of clients, the majority of clients I've worked with, I think, would focus on the, on what needed to be done and do that and evaluate that. And in that, I mean, it sounds so obvious, like, well, of course, right, but it's not that obvious because then there was another significant group of clients I worked with that were, they were always far less interested in what got done, and more interested in what could have been done, the things they could have done. And it took me a long time, and it was, it was incredibly frustrating as a service provider to come in, you know, having established a strategy, executed that strategy, had a result that was positive. And then to sit down with either an executive or an executive team, or business owner, and then have them focus on things that we didn't do, that we never talked about doing, that didn't necessarily make sense to do it, right. Like, it was, it was strange to observe, and I think this is just, it may just be how some of us are wired, right, and it's not bad, it's not good. It's, it's just what I would consider suboptimal and counterproductive, because if the goal, if you're, I'm going to make some assumptions and all externalize the assumption so you, you'll know how I'm thinking about this, but if the goal in purchasing and going and doing the work in the momentum building a workshop was to build moment, right, was to make progress towards something, then that's where the focus should be, is just sit down, say to yourself, I'm going to do this thing, and believe that this thing is going to get me closer to what I'm trying to accomplish. The moment we start thinking about all the things we're not doing, when we're that we could do, we're just, we're just, we derail progress immediately. And this is, it's common, like what you're experiencing, and again, I'm making up so many assumptions about what I think you're experiencing, but I've seen this so many times, I think you're experiencing this, where it's like there's there's a path in front of you that you could walk on, but the moment you commit to that path, you are excluding everything else that you could do. So you have to trust that that path is the right path, and maybe it's not maybe your intuition is correct, maybe you've looked at the momentum to build a workshop and thought you know what, looking back this isn't the right choice for me at this time. Maybe you know a podcast would be a better idea or and if that's the case that's the case and we, you know, we I'm not suggesting that you're wrong, but I would encourage everyone listening to this to ask yourself, why am I looking outside of the scope of what I'm focused on? Am I, am I concerned? Am I procrastinating? Do I not is something not clear enough? Do I not see how this thing that I'm trying to do connects the dots between where I am and what I'm trying to accomplish like really ask yourself those questions. And if the answer is, you know what this this workshop that I purchased, actually if I did it would get me closer to where I'm trying to go, then do it. Do the work. And don't focus on anything else, because the this is the, I've said this elsewhere. So I apologize if you've heard me rant about this already, but this is there's there's a funny question that gets asked in and I'm sure this is true, well beyond internet marketing circles but in our corner of the world there's a funny little switch with questions that happens. And here's what, here's the switch. Most people ask the question, particularly agencies, this is the, if you ever run an agency, this is the agency trick. When a company is working with an agency, another company comes in, and they show the company that they're, that they're trying to attract. They put together these massive reports of everything that they could be doing, like could, that's the key word. So you know you could be blogging you could be podcasting you could be doing video, and you're not doing any of these things so the agency you're working with is, they can't be very good because you could be doing so much that you're not doing. Right. The problem with could is it assumes that time energy and dollars are infinite. And there are millions of things that we could do. But in the real world, time energy and dollars are finite, which means that we have to ask the question, should versus could. So, given the given the amount of time, energy and dollars I have to spend, and the goal that I'm trying to achieve. What should I do. Well that eliminates 99.9% of your choice, percent of your choices immediately, because if you're just getting started, which is really the focus of bringing you know an idea out into the world that's the focus of the momentum built workshop. If you're, if you're just getting started and trying to make contact between an audience and an offer. Podcasting probably is not your best option. Could you do it. Absolutely. You could also do another hundred thousand other different things. But should you do it. Well, my answer to that question would be no. Not in every situation there certainly context where that's not true. But from the, from the realization that they're everything has opportunity costs. I would, I would focus on the thing that gets me from that connects my offer with an audience as quickly as possible. So I hope that all makes some kind of sense to others, aside from me. Anything to add to that Andre before we move on. Yeah, I think that's you've, you've, you've made a good point there. You know, it's, it's one thing to, to layer to layer on extras later on, once, once you once you have traction, you know, that's, that's, that's the, that's the way businesses, you know, work, right. But you need to get traction first. And it's impossible to get traction when, when you fleet from one thing to the next thing to the next thing. You know, God knows I've done that many times. And when I was trying to figure out this stuff, never quite knowing if what I'm doing is going to work and if I should double down with it or try something else. You know, it's, it's the classic conundrum, but you know, there's, there's a lot of value in just believing that something is going to work and you just do it until you've got it working. And, you know, once you have that traction, you know, double down on it, get it working to the point where you satisfied and then layer on the next thing, you know, prioritize what do you think the next thing is that's going to improve everything. And, you know, maybe at some point, it is a podcast. But it certainly wouldn't be a podcast in the beginning if there's nothing there. Yeah. Okay, so I think that's. But what I just said, I know certainly from my perspective, came from many, many, many times of making the wrong choices. Right, of doing exactly what I was describing. I mean, I've been down this road personally, plenty of times where I've, you know, had, I wanted to do something. And I orchestrated every conceivable way not to actually do that thing, because the moment you put a stake in the ground and do something, you're going to get a result. And most of the time, the result that you get is not going to be ideal. That's just, that's reality. So, and none of us want, you know, fail is the wrong word, but it's the word that culturally we hear a lot. None of us want to fail. And the best way to guarantee that you never fail is to never put anything out into the world. Right, always be in the process of doing something. Right, so if you if you want to have a, if you want to have a, it's not really a business to generate money, it'll be an expensive hobby. But if you want to have an expensive hobby, where you don't have to fail, that's possible. It's easy to engineer that I've done that many times where I've, I've always been in the process of executing an idea that before the idea actually was executed, I had a different idea and I could, I could keep it going, and not have to actually put something out into the world and get a result. But the moment that you recognize that when you do put something out in the world, whatever happens is just a result. And it's a piece of information. And I realize that sounds trite like, Oh, but what if I put my heart and soul into that. And that part of the reason we've designed the momentum builder workshop the way we have is to rush people through it so you have to put words on screens and get work done, so that you can eventually get a result that will most likely be suboptimal. But the moment you get that result, you can take a step back and sort of assess what it means, and then do make a change to do something different. And it doesn't guarantee the result will be better, but it will be different. And then you keep doing that. But you can't do that. If you haven't connected an audience with an offer, then everything is just it's sort of intellectual, you're imagining what might happen, like our fear gets in the way, our monkey mind gets in the way. And it prevents us from actually getting to the other side of the thing that's going to make all the difference. And that's really what we hope to create with a momentum builder workshop is let's let's short circuit that thing that is preventing people from just getting version one out into the world because the moment version one exists. The foundation has been set for version two. And more importantly, the foundation has been set for version 12, because it may take it may take version 12 before all of a sudden you think this is this is really starting to work. Right so that question there. It just becomes a question of how do you how do you find that audience right that audience probably doesn't stumble on to you with $50,000 things on Facebook. I just don't think so but when we get into the other numbers. You know I've seen people sell $15,000 programs business building programs. I mean, you know, I'm a business owner and a Facebook ad. It wasn't because of this is something Andre and I have talked about repeated. There is no causal relationship we've ever seen between an auto the structure of, quote unquote a funnel or a business or a system, and the output the result right so there's no, I know this is this is one to shock some of you, based on what everybody is doing. There's no way to do what we do functionally structurally technical technologically that guarantees a result. There's a just isn't because at the end of the day marketing always comes down to two things offer an audience. If you put the right offer in front of the right audience, and everything else. It doesn't matter what I mean, you can make a million mistakes and you still get a good result. If you put the right offer in front of the right audience or the, you know, right off from from the wrong audience you can figure out the different permutations here. It just doesn't work. Right so that leads me to start thinking about the question of how, how does the momentum builder workshop connect the right audience to the right audience. And I think that's the better way to think about this, like, does it do that, and how does it do that and the answer is yes. It's that's how it's built. I mean it's built to get the attention of the right audience meaning we're not using curiosity to get clicks because we want to get, you know 1000 leads, hoping that we have 10 customers in there, we're not playing the volume game, the quantity game. So, immediately we're, we're able to attract fewer people of higher quality, which, again, if that, depending on what the eventual offer is higher quality is always better. That's just, I think that's something that we would all agree to that if all things being equal, would you rather have 10 high quality prospects, or 10 low quality prospects, we're all going to say high quality like that's that's, there's no rationality. There's no rationale to say the opposite. So, knowing that, I guess the way I would think about this question is, where does the, where does price factor in like how does price of the eventual offer relate to the component parts. So, would, would price affect the Facebook ad, sort of, because you would be, you would be speaking to a much smaller audience in a Facebook ad, when you know that the offer that you're eventually going to present is $50,000 or 15,000 or 5000. You know that already. So you would, your Facebook ad would change and that it would be, you would be way more interesting in weeding out people who are not going to be the right prospects. So that, that might change a little bit. The landing page itself, probably a similar thing. I mean, I've just, I mean, just been running this through my head, figuring, trying to figure out scenarios in which somebody could sell a $50,000 product for as much stuff, like as much effort, you know, in terms of how many words, you know, has a 50 or 500. And I think there are examples of that. And I think it's going to be, you know, as we move into the future, into this weird crypto world, I can totally, I mean, see, see somebody with an NFT offer on, on a, you know, getting interest, you know, using a Facebook ad, putting in front of the right people who are investing in, in, in, in NFT JPEGs, sitting at two very simple landing page, and there's an auction and next in a $50,000 JPEG has just been sold, you know, just like that, or $1 million JPEG has just been sold and in a very simple structure of bringing somebody in. It's just, as you mentioned earlier on, it's about connecting an audience with an offer. And if you can find the right audience and connect them to the right offer. And price is price almost becomes irrelevant because price is, is subject to what an audience is willing to pay. And if, if, if somebody has a budget of millions to to bar, you know, NFT JPEGs, 50,000 is is just a drop in the bucket they'll, they'll spend that without even betting an eyelid. And so, I think they are, they are certainly use cases where you wouldn't, you know, somebody selling a JPEG that cost that much money wouldn't require 10,000 words of, of, or 100,000 words of copy, I don't think that's the case. In fact, I can see it being even less somebody buying images and probably doesn't want to read that much. It's just really to the point, they know what they want. It's crystal clear in their mind. They're, they're in the market for investing in, in these JPEGs, and probably just a little bit of text in a video does the trick, does the trick. And there we go. So I think, yeah, that's an interesting something that you just said hints at something or points to something really interesting to and I'd love to draw this someday. But I think it's a bell curve. Right. So the horizontal axis is cost. Right. So if you can imagine an XY axis is horizontal axis is is the price, right, the price of the offer. And then the vertical axis is, is how much do you have to give in exchange for that. And what's, what's interesting is, and maybe it's not a bell curve, I would have any, I want to spend some time with this. It may be a linear curve or maybe some sort of weird distribution, but it's, there's a there's a funny thing that happens with with price. At the very low end, low to, you know, like the 50 to 500 range, the relationship between price and in what you deliver in exchange is enormous. Right. There's this expectation of, you know, there's million bonuses and, you know, it's, it's, it's so outsized value relative to the cost. But the farther you go to the right, and particularly when you go really far to the right on that axis toward the really high end. A funny thing happens that people want less and less and less stuff. And they want more and more focus. Right. So when you get these when you in the end, there's also another thing that goes on, especially in a sort of a training context or education context is that the importance of relationships of the group becomes really important to and it's less about the content. So this is, this is it and I don't know where this observation fits in exactly but it certainly suggests that there's not a linear increase necessary in what you communicate to people. So that it's not like, well, if it's $50, if I'm selling something for $50, I need, you know, 500 words will do it. And if it's $500, then I need 5000 words to do it. And then if it's, you know, 15,000, it's 50, it's not that at all. It's more like if you're selling a 50 a $50 thing so let's look at financial newsletter publishers for example companies like the Motley Fool and a Gora financial and legacy research. You know, all these these companies, the lowest their their front end offer is generally a $49 newsletter, and they sell those newsletters with 36,000 word sales letters. So if you think about that 36,000 words, or, you know, let's call it somewhere between 20 and 30,000 20 and 1000 20 to 30,000 word sales letters to sell a $50 product. Right. And it's shocking. But then when you eventually get up to their back end offers that it's not enough you don't, you don't get another sales letter. That's another 30,000 words and it's, it's interesting to think about that that it takes. It takes often a lot more to sell an offer to that's lower cost to an audience than to sell a high cost offer. That's weird to think about. So getting back to Brian's question. I know I talk a lot here but I think the way to think about this question is not. I wouldn't use that framework. I think that's really that's a long way of the answer is no. Right, like, it's not. It's not like those two dimensions. Right. So the dimension that Brian has has inferred a relationship, which I don't think is accurate. In a sense, it's a brilliant question. And it's really fun to think about we can go miles deep into this question but the relationship he's inferred is that there's a something changes in the structure of how we communicate with an offer with an audience as prices increase logarithmically. And I don't, I don't think that's the relationship. I think things change, but they don't change that way. So it's not a matter of like, well, I've got this $50 thing so I need to do this much. But now that I'm doing, I had a $500 off, I got to do these other things. It's probably, it's probably some it's somewhere in the 50 to 500 probably have similar ish things that you need to do. And then when you get up to the $5,000 range. It's the selling part that becomes it's it's the match between audience and offer and how you how you show that and demonstrate that match. And do you get the connection right like the real emotional need. Like that's at the core of all marketing anyway but like that becomes way more important and filtering out the wrong audience becomes way more important. And as you continue to go up the scale of pricing, I think what really begins to matter more is not the metric of the structure of the system or how much content you share, or what the nature of that content is it's really how well do you connect the needs of that audience which is much smaller to the value that you create which is far more focused. I think that's actually the way to think about it. Okay, good time. That was fun. What a question. Yeah, that's a good question. But the, what the momentum builder workshop does really well is what successful businesses do really well, which is identify the shortest path shortest reasonable path between a to b audience and offer. Craft something that most likely does not work iterate that thing until it starts to work, and then don't complicate it more than is necessary to keep getting a result. And you can, you can take the momentum builder workshop the lessons in the momentum builder workshop and build a very sophisticated very productive, very successful business, just using that framework you don't have to do something else. If you find yourself wanting to do something else than yes, absolutely. You can build whole systems with with the art of email and with sphere of influence and with traffic engine, you put them all together you can do even more complex things but don't let the possibility of complexity and the attractiveness of complexity interfere with the power of simplicity. The momentum builder workshop is deliberately simple, it is not simplistic, it is simple, and simple gets results fast complex, not so much. It can, but complexity often gets in the way of the effectiveness of a simple solution. So, stay focused on what the momentum builder workshop doesn't don't get ahead of yourself. No, that's not at all we're talking about you're building a relationship in the context of the value that you create for your audience, and that caught in that begins with empathy, the reflection that you understand the pain that they feel and the desire that they want. Oftentimes because you've experienced that same pain and have that same desire. And it goes from empathy to then credibility, gently, not necessarily a step by step thing, but all it can blend there can be empathy and authority can overlap and credibility they can sort of overlap one another in many ways and oftentimes things that demonstrate empathy by an emergent property is credibility like if someone has felt your pain, and they've done something that has helped them solve that problem that that's empathy and credibility together we just don't have a word for that in the English language. I don't think that combines those two things. But the context of the of the relationship is always always always framed by the ad expanded upon by the landing page, and then it's extended it's really extended over days in those emails so someone raises is or her hand, because you're at your ad is framing a conversation we'll use Jonathan Baker as an example. So, when Jonathan Baker who was a cycling coach for elite cyclists who want to go faster on a bicycle. If Jonathan were frame sort of frames the case in his Facebook ad, and his landing page that they're really only three ways to go faster on a bicycle. These are his words not ours. But in Jonathan's experience and he has a lot of it 30 plus years as a PhD, and believe in exercise physiology or something very sophisticated tremendous amount of experience, but the context that people are entering that are seeing the relationship building emails with him. The context is that they want to go faster on a bicycle. And he has explained to them that there are three ways to do that. And, and he's going to expand upon and those three ways, you can make your bicycle lighter, you can become more aerodynamic, or you can generate more power as a cyclist. And the first two are you often very expensive and very little benefit there's just not a lot of there there. So, the real opportunity is in jet and learning how to generate more power, and having a training plan that helps you generate more power, which happens to be the thing that that Jonathan has devoted his career toward. So when, when the relationship building starts, it's a relationship that's built between is that's being built between somebody who is a cyclist who wants to go faster on a bicycle, and someone who spent a career, helping people go faster on a bicycle. Jonathan may introduce some other things tangentially that contributes to you know sort of that relationship but the core of the relationship is built around this common interest. That's what all of the relationship buildings happens about it's not just, we're not saying take a step back and like hey let's get to know Jonathan Baker, it's not it at all. It's always in context, and always in service of the eventual offer that you're going to make the offer creates value for your audience is that's who we're focusing on someone has entered our world. Right. And the consequences. They're just they're making their decisions without considering all of the consequences. And that's in if we if we all do that, then there's no reason not to send it everybody. Right, but here's the problem. Attention, the attention that our audience gives us is not infinite. It is the most precious resource that they have. And when we show people to when we show offers to people who are not interested, we train them to stop paying attention to us. So the question I would ask your clients is, do we think it's a viable intelligent long term strategy to train our clients, not to pay attention to what we send to them. Or do we think it is a better strategy in the short and long term to train our clients to pay attention to us. Right, because the bridge email does two things beautifully. It, it rewards attention. You raise your hand to get something a lot more than others do. And it protects in attention. And if we think about the members of our audience have this finite amount of attention that they can give to everything in a day, only certain number of hours a day some units of energy we have to pay attention to things. If we are, if we send them a lot of inputs that they're not interested in, we make it easier to ignore us over time. But if we send them things. And when they don't demonstrate interest. We don't keep sending them those things unless they say they want it. Then when we send them something, they're paying, we were, they're more likely to pay attention. That's how I would frame this conversation with them. And, you know, at the end of the day, the other way to frame this to and this gets you to sort of lead to getting better clients is positioning yourself as the expert, and you can say, I absolutely understand why that makes more sense. Like, I absolutely understand why you would think that sending to everyone all the time makes sense because the, it doesn't appear that the cost that there's any cost to do that, because it doesn't cost more money to send more emails. But there is a cost. And in my experience, this is how I would frame it if I were you, in my experience, there is a very significant cost but that cost doesn't show up in any particular metric, or it doesn't show up on the balance sheet, because the way that cost is paid for the audience can learning to stop paying attention to us. So if we send everything to everybody all the time, we're going to change the vast, we're going to train the vast majority of our audience to gradually over time pay less and less attention until they just unsubscribe. And that's not in our best interest. So I can't recommend to you. You, and I've said this a million times to clients, you were, you were hiring me for my expertise and my experience, and my expertise and experience says that you will find far more value. If you send emails to people who have actively shown interest as a practice that that's indisputable, because of the cost paid in attention that will cause people to gradually over time pay less attention to us. That's how I would frame the whole conversation. And if they say, Oh, that's, that's great, but do it anyway, then put that person on the top of your list to be one of the clients that goes away when you get a better client. Because really, I mean, part of being in the client services business is continually upgrading your clients. And at first, and I've been there, I've worked, believe me, I've worked for way longer than I want to admit with clients that were not high, not great clients, I banged my head against that wall more times than I can count. But I'm not suggesting that if you have a certain number of clients and they're helping you pay the bills that you go to them say, you know what's my way to the highway. If you don't want to do it this way, you're gone. But go to the client, make your case. If the client still wants to do it a different way, put them on the list of clients to replace eventually when you get better clients. And then when you're, when you're looking for new clients, look for clients where you're positioning yourself as an expert for them to rely on. And then you want to attract a client that will respect your decision making. And that client gets to when the time is right and you have your begin to have fewer and fewer hours. But what I, what I realized is looking back is that there was this weird oscillation where they would get some new director of marketing, and I would have to start over and I think I basically trained four or five of their directors of marketing over the years, but it was a pain. They would come in and they would want to do a lot of dumb things and then I would have to say that's not how we do it. This is how we do it and this is why. And then, but they were often six months, nine months, a year or more of within the larger relationship me having to do that. So when it got time to say, you know, it's time for us to part ways, it was an incredible relief. And they really couldn't pay me more and there's, there's a funny thing too about real, this is another one of those weird relationships, the relationship between how much you get paid, and how accommodating you are is inverse, right, which is weird. And I think that you would get paid way more to be agreeable, but agreeable service providers are a dime a dozen, you can go find that's a race to the bottom you can go find lots of them on five, five or you can find lots of them who will do what they're, they're told on the other side of the book and other things like they're a dime a dozen, but the person who looks you in the eye and says, I know more about this than you do. And I, in the way you're making decisions, you're not saying this exactly, but you're the way you carry yourself, and the way you present how you do I know more about this than you do. That's why you've hired me. And I'm going to be very clear about how we need to do this to get the best result that you want. And when you do things that are stupid, or you do things that are counter to your interest because you don't know better. I'm going to be very clear about what we need to do and I'm not going to back down from that. I'm not doing it to be a jerk. I'm doing it because when I can just gets back to the framing of what you're really doing which is very clearly identified at the outcomes that they want, and you're making it clear that you're working to those outcomes and you know more about how to get there than they do. So that's that's when you start, you make infinitely more either there's a logarithmic increase in the amount of money that you make as a consultant, the less agreeable you become which is really strange and it's not to be clear it's not about being agreeable disagreeable it's about having a position and sticking to that position that isn't your client's best interest. But again, I'm just thinking about larger because I've never done this but if you go into the effort of building your entire funnel out and then you're buying Facebook ads, you're buying traffic. Yeah, it's going to be a hell of a lot more than 15%. It's going to be more than 50% and a lot more. I mean look at the whole affiliate structure, right? I mean people are like, and this is, how many thousands of times have you heard someone say like, you know, I can't believe that you're paying an affiliate 50%. That's crazy. It's like, at what point is it crazy to get 50% of a sale you wouldn't have had otherwise? Like, it makes it like that argument is crazy. Like, it's the same. I think it's a very similar argument to Airbnb. It's like, why in the world would you pay 15% to someone who's going to do all of the marketing for you? You just answered your own question, right? Like, I would happily pay Airbnb 15% to not have to do all of this complex other stuff. There are exceptions sometimes, but I think if Richard Kosh said this, the 80-20 guy said this, it's the horse, not the rider. Airbnb is the horse. It's the platform, right? It's driving, it's aggregating an enormous audience looking for rental properties. And it has all of the tools to connect those people with what they're looking for, where they're looking for, across all these dimensions you can't even imagine. And it only costs 15% to use that. It costs you money only after you've made the sale, right? What could be more perfect? Because Facebook doesn't charge you if you make a sale. They charge you. If you don't make a sale, you still get charged. The simple answer here in this specific example is don't overthink when a platform does exactly what you want it to do, which is aggregate an audience for you and direct that audience, point that audience in the direction that you're offered. Airbnb does that exceptionally well. Zillow does that exceptionally well. And you can benefit from those. If you have an offer that matches up to a platform like that, use it. That's my advice. All right, that's all the questions. Yeah, good. Two hours, just sharp, two hours. Nice. Well, thanks everybody for attending the call. We have a small group, but still a fun group. I appreciate everybody's questions. If you heard an answer to your question, you're like, wow, that seemed a little tough. Hey, it's tough love, right? We like you guys a lot. And if we didn't, we could give you half-assed answers that we felt like would make you happy. And we're not. We are in the happy customer business. Sometimes like, but we're in the long-term happy customer business, which we don't want to tell you something that we know through experience is going to lead you in a direction that's not ideal. So when you get some tough love, recognize both parts of that phrase, tough and love, we care about all of you. So when we answer your questions, we're answering your questions. I know I can speak for both of us, but I'll speak for myself here that we're answering questions. It's oftentimes how we would tell ourselves the same thing, knowing what we know, and often having made a lot of mistakes. I know the wisdom that I've gained over the years came at the expense of a tremendous number of wrong turns and mistakes, which I, if I could wish something for all of you, it was just not to make as many mistakes as I did.
